title: What is a structure used for

content.problemStatement:
A structure in the C++ programming language is used to group together variables of different data types under a single name. \
It allows you to create more complex data structures that can represent real-world objects or concepts in your program. \
Structures are commonly used for various purposes:
- Organizing Related Data: Structures are used to group related data that belongs together.\
For example, if you're working with coordinates, you can create a structure to hold both the x and y values of a point.
- Creating Records: Structures are often used to define records or objects that have multiple attributes. \
For instance, you might create a structure to represent a person's information with attributes like name, age, and address.
- Passing Complex Data: Structures can be used to pass complex data between functions. \
Instead of passing individual variables, you can pass a structure containing all the necessary data, making the code more organized and easier to understand.
- Representing Real-world Entities: Structures are helpful when you want to represent real-world entities in your program. \
For instance, you could use a structure to model a car with attributes like make, model, year, and color.
- Data Storage: Structures are used in situations where you need to store and manage multiple pieces of data as a single unit. \
For example, when dealing with data records in a database or when reading and writing data to and from files.
- Implementing Linked Lists and Trees: Data structures like linked lists, trees, and graphs often use structures to define the nodes that make up these data structures.

Overall, structures provide a way to organize, manage, and manipulate complex data in a more intuitive and logical manner. \
They are fundamental building blocks that contribute to the organization, readability, and maintainability of C++ programs.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 10^3$
- $1 \leq N \leq 10^3$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ over all testcases does not exceed $2\cdot 10^3$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of two lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains one integer $N$, denoting the number of cities
    - The second line contains $2N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2N}$ — the elements of the array $A$.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case output the answer on a new line — `Yes` if the given array represents a valid list of prices, and `No` otherwise.

Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, `Yes`, `YES`, and `yEs` will all be treated as equivalent.
